Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-002 — Ru-type Vase with Sky-Blue Glaze

Reference Object ro-song-002

Ru-type Vase with Sky-Blue Glaze and Crackle

汝窑型 — Northern Song Dynasty, late 11th – early 12th century

Northern Song Ru Ware Sky-Blue Glaze Phase Separation Fish Scale Effect GOM Examined Burial Alteration

Object Description

A vase of elongated form with a flared mouth, compressed globular shoulder, and cylindrical body tapering to a slightly splayed foot ring. The glaze is of the classic Ru sky-blue tone — pale greyish-blue with a soft internal luminosity traditionally described as “sky after rain” (雨過天青). The glaze surface carries a fine crackle network distributed uniformly across all glazed surfaces, including the glazed interior of the foot.

The foot ring is unglazed, revealing the characteristic buff-grey stoneware body of Northern Song Ru production. The glaze terminates cleanly above the foot ring with a slightly thickened accumulation zone at the lower body, consistent with original kiln firing orientation.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The vase was examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) under reflected and oblique illumination at magnifications from 20× to 200×. The transparency of Ru glaze allows internal microstructural features to be observed directly through the intact glaze surface without sampling or preparation. Observations document firing technology, crystallisation processes, and long-term burial ageing. Results were compared with published instrumental studies of authenticated Northern Song Ru ware.

The transparency of authentic Ru glaze is itself a diagnostic indicator: it permits direct optical access to internal microstructural features including phase separation domains, anorthite crystal reflections, and entrapped microbubbles — all visible without any destructive preparation.

GOM Documentation — Glaze Microstructure

~50×

Fig. 4. Fish Scale Effect (魚鱗紋). Overlapping arcuate segments with gentle undulating stratification record glaze movement during cooling. This texture cannot be artificially reproduced.

~100×

Fig. 5. Liquid–liquid phase separation. Pale bluish rounded domains within a darker blue-grey interstitial matrix. Bimodal microstructure characteristic of authentic Ru glaze.

GOM Documentation — Body–Glaze Interface and Foot Ring

~80×

Fig. 6. Body–glaze interface. Matte whitish alteration zones (Dry Earth Effect) and corrosion halos are visible as evidence of long-term burial weathering.

~60×

Fig. 7. Foot ring profile under oblique illumination. Multiple spur marks with post-firing grinding traces and glaze flow accumulation consistent with Northern Song imperial firing technology.

GOM Documentation — Firing Evidence

~150×

Fig. 8. Spur support marks at higher magnification. Original firing geometry preserved; no secondary mechanical alteration. Consistent with documented Northern Song Ru kiln practice.

1. Liquid–Liquid Phase Separation

The most important microscopic feature is the direct optical observation of liquid–liquid phase separation within the glaze. Microscopy reveals a bimodal microstructure composed of pale bluish rounded domains within a darker blue-grey interstitial matrix, distributed irregularly throughout the glaze with continuous internal optical texture. These structures correspond to silica-rich and calcium-rich glass phases produced during cooling and represent one of the principal diagnostic characteristics of authentic Ru glaze.

2. Anorthite Crystal Reflections

Under oblique illumination, numerous elongated reflective features become visible beneath the glaze surface. These needle-shaped structures with irregular orientation and heterogeneous distribution produce strong directional reflections corresponding to buried anorthite crystals formed during crystallisation of the CaO–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ glaze system. Their presence confirms the original high-temperature crystallisation process rather than modern glaze manufacture.

3. Entrapped Microbubbles

Microscopy demonstrates numerous enclosed spherical gas bubbles preserved within the glaze matrix, variable in diameter and completely encapsulated with optical scattering visible around each bubble. These microbubbles contribute to the jade-like appearance of Ru glaze by increasing internal light scattering and their distribution is consistent with high-temperature reduction firing.

4. Internal Opalescence

The glaze exhibits a distinct internal luminous quality rather than simple surface gloss. This optical effect results from the combined action of liquid–liquid phase separation, anorthite crystallisation, and entrapped gas bubbles producing multiple light scattering. The resulting appearance corresponds to the traditional description of Ru glaze as “sky after rain” (雨過天青).

5. Fish Scale Effect — 魚鱗紋

Oblique illumination reveals overlapping arcuate segments with gentle undulating stratification and soft boundaries indicating directional flow structures. Rather than representing decoration, this texture records glaze movement during cooling and is regarded as an important diagnostic marker because of its complex technological origin and the practical impossibility of artificial reproduction.

6. Crackle Continuity

The crackle network continues uninterrupted across all glazed surfaces, including the glazed interior of the foot. Identical crack morphology, continuous propagation, uniform ageing, and integrated structural behaviour throughout the object reflect original glaze formation rather than artificially induced crackling.

7. Spur Support Marks

The foot preserves multiple original spur marks with post-firing grinding traces, glaze flow accumulation, and authentic firing geometry. These features correspond to documented Northern Song imperial firing technology and have not been subjected to secondary mechanical alteration.

8. Dry Earth Effect (DEE)

One of the most significant observations is the presence of the Dry Earth Effect. Microscopy reveals matte whitish surface zones with preferential alteration around phase-separated domains and corrosion halos surrounding microbubbles, indicating progressive surface degradation. These features represent the earliest optical manifestation of long-term burial corrosion, recording the gradual dissolution of calcium-rich phase-separated domains.

9. Corrosion Halos Around Microbubbles

Many enclosed bubbles are surrounded by concentric corrosion halos indicating heterogeneous weathering, preferential chemical alteration, and progressive degradation of the surrounding glass matrix. This phenomenon is consistent with naturally aged archaeological Ru glaze and differs fundamentally from modern reproductions in both distribution and morphology.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The authentication of this object is based on the agreement of multiple independent microscopic indicators. Together these observations reconstruct the complete technological history of the glaze, from crystallisation during firing to natural alteration over many centuries. No individual feature alone proves authenticity; their internal consistency forms a robust diagnostic profile.

The coexistence of primary firing features (phase separation, anorthite crystals, microbubbles, Fish Scale Effect, spur marks) with secondary ageing phenomena (Dry Earth Effect, corrosion halos) that are themselves consistent in distribution and intensity constitutes the strongest available microscopic authentication evidence for Northern Song Ru ware.

Comparative References

Published instrumental studies of authenticated Northern Song Ru ware using SEM–EDS, XRF, and XRD, including analyses of phase separation and anorthite crystallisation in the CaO–Al₂O₃–SiO₂ glaze system, were consulted for comparative calibration of GOM observations. Full citations to be added.
